CZ_NAME: LARIMER COUNTY BELOW 6000 FEET / NW WELD COUNTY
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Arctic air moved into eastern Colorado the 23rd and linger into the 24th. A brief warmup occurred on the 25th ahead of a second arctic cold front produced falling temperatures during the afternoon of the 25th. Light snow occurred on the 23rd and the 25th. Total snow generally ranged up to 2 inches, with a few locations seeing 3 inches. Wind chill readings fell into -15F to -25F during the morning of the 23rd, only improving later on the 24th into the 25th. Wind chills again fell into the -15F to -25F range later on the 25th and during the early morning hours of the 26th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind chills readings fell to -15 to -25F.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure set up across western Colorado overnight from January 3 to 4, leading to clear skies and light winds. Pools of strong moisture remained within many valleys though from precipitation earlier in January. With this, cooling overnight temperatures led to a saturated near-surface environment and widespread dense fog.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread dense fog developed within valleys along I-70 during the morning of January 4. The KRIL ASOS at the Rifle Garfield County Airport reported 1/4-mile visibility due to dense fog for about a half an hour between 5:30-6:00 AM. After a brief period of improved visibilities, dense fog redeveloped around 7:00 AM. Visibilities reached 1/4 mile or less for about 3.5 hours before dissipating. No reports of injuries, damages, or fatalities were received.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broad trough moved through the CONUS in mid-January with weak flow situated over Colorado. Embedded shortwaves moved through the trough, bringing bouts of active weather, including overnight from January 8 to 9. Strong snow bands developed ahead of the cold front moving through southwestern Colorado. As skies cleared and temperatures dropped behind the front, dense fog developed within some valleys near the San Juan mountains where heavy snow had occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread fog, including areas of dense fog, developed within the Animas River Basin during the morning of January 9 following heavy snowfall. The KDRO ASOS at the Durango La-Plata County Airport reported two prolonged periods of visibility being reduced to or below 1 mile. During the first period, sporadic and brief periods of 1/4-mile visibility due to dense fog occurred. During the second period which began shortly before sunrise, dense fog reduced visibility to or less than 1/4 mile for about three and a half hours before slowly dissipating through mid-morning. No reports of injuries, damages, or fatalities were received.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The winter of 2025-2026 has been, overall, a dry and warm one. And as such, drought has clung on across eastern Utah and western Colorado, especially across the Central Divide Mountains. Areas in the eastern Uinta mountains have also seen drought linger.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At the beginning of the month of January, the US Drought Monitor analyzed an area of Extreme (D3) Drought covering approximately 3% of Colorado Zone 001 - Lower Yampa River Basin. The remainder of the zone saw 27% Severe (D2) Drought and 70% Moderate (D1) Drought. With the 1/7 US Drought Monitor update, the area of extreme (D3) Drought was removed from the zone, with drought now 30% Severe (D2) and 70% Moderate (D1) in the zone. No reports of damages or impacts from this drought have been received.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The winter of 2025-2026 has been, overall, a dry and warm one. And as such, drought has clung on across eastern Utah and western Colorado, especially across the Central Divide Mountains. Areas in the eastern Uinta mountains have also seen drought linger.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At the beginning of the month of January, the US Drought Monitor analyzed an area of Exceptional (D4) Drought covering 5% of Colorado Zone 008 - Central Colorado River Basin. Extreme (D3) Drought covered an additional 40% of the zone, with Severe (D2) covering the remainder. With the 1/21 US Drought Monitor update, the area of Extreme (D3) Drought expanded to cover roughly 85% of the zone, with the coverage of Severe (D2) Drought shrinking to just 10% of the zone. The 5% coverage of Exceptional (D4) Drought remained unchanged. No reports of damages or impacts have been received.

CZ_NAME: GORE AND ELK MOUNTAINS/CENTRAL MOUNTAIN VALLEYS
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The winter of 2025-2026 has been, overall, a dry and warm one. And as such, drought has clung on across eastern Utah and western Colorado, especially across the Central Divide Mountains. Areas in the eastern Uinta mountains have also seen drought linger.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At the beginning of the month of January, the US Drought Monitor analyzed an area of Exceptional (D4) Drought as covering roughly 25% of Colorado Zone 010 - Gore and Elk Mountains/Central Mountain Valleys. Extreme (D3) Drought covered an additional 40% of the zone, Severe (D2) Drought covered 48% of the zone, and Moderate (D1) Drought covered just 2% of the zone. With the 1/7 Drought Monitor update, the area of Exceptional (D4) Drought increased to 30%, while the area of Severe (D2) Drought dropped to 44% and Moderate (D1) Drought dropped to 1% coverage. Extreme (D3) Drought coverage remained unchanged. With the 1/21 update, Exceptional (D4) Drought expanded again to 35% coverage, with Extreme (D3) Drought expanding to 45% coverage, with 19% Severe (D2) and 1% Moderate (D1) covering the remainder. No reports of damages or impacts have been recieved.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moved through northeast Colorado on the evening of the 15th. Multiple wind gusts ranging from 55 mph - 65 mph were recorded. Blowing dust with visibility around 0.10 miles reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At 1 E Vona, blowing dust reported with visibilities approximately 0.10 mile.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moved through northeast Colorado on the evening of the 15th. Multiple wind gusts ranging from 55 mph - 65 mph were recorded. Blowing dust with visibility around 0.10 miles reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A UPR at 2 E Arapahoe reported wind gusts of 64 mph, 63 (x2) mph and 62 mph. A personal weather station at 2 SSW Firstview reported a 59 mph gust. A UPR at 1 WNW Kit Carson recorded a gust of 59 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As the month of January continued to be generally drier and warmer than normal, drought began to expand outward from the core of Exceptional (D4) Drought in the central Colorado Divide.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: With the 1/21 update of the US Drought Monitor, Extreme (D3) Drought had developed in Colorado Zone 004 - Elkhead and Park Mountains. This area of Extreme (D3) Drought covered 25% of the zone, with 40% of the zone under Severe (D2) Drought conditions, and the remaining 35% of the zone under Moderate (D1) Drought conditions. These conditions remained unchanged through the end of the month. No reports of impacts or damages related to the drought in this zone have been received.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A jet streak and shortwave trough pushed through the northern and central portions of the area, bringing moderate snowfall to the northern and central Colorado mountains as well as the Eastern Uinta Mountains of northeast Utah. The strong southwest flow also led to windy conditions at the higher elevations resulting in blowing snow as well. Temperatures stayed warm with this system, keeping better snow accumulations mainly confined to areas above 9000 feet.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 5 to 10 inches of snow fell across the area with up to 11 inches at Tower. A peak wind gust of 46 mph occurred at Storm Peak Lab.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front swept southward across eastern Colorado during the evening of January 15th, with it bringing wind gusts of 55 to 70 mph. Winds subsided overnight, but became strong again during morning and afternoon hours of January 16th. A few power poles came down in Larimer County. Several miles of I-25 north of Fort Collins to the WY state line was closed to all traffic and US 85 north of Greeley to the WY line was closed to all traffic as well. A grass fire (about 500 acres) occurred along I-76 in Sedgwick county south of Sedgwick. Mandatory evacuations south of I-76 and east of HWY 59 lasted about hour before the fire was contained.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds generally reach 40 to 60 mph across the foothills of Larimer County (below the 75 mph for high wind criteria). Even weaker winds were reported in the foothills of Boulder County. A small corridor near the Wyoming border experienced strong winds with the strongest report being 78 mph near Virginia Dale.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An open wave low pressure trough moved through Utah and formed a closed low near the Four Corners before pushing through southwest Colorado. Significant moisture ahead of this trough and cold air behind a strong cold front led to moderate to heavy snowfall across the mountains of western Colorado and southeast Utah, with the highest snowfall totals across the Uncompahgre Gorge area of the northwest San Juans due to favorable light northerly flow behind the closed low and temperatures within the dendritic growth zone.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 6 to 15 inches of snow fell across the area with locally higher amounts up to 16.5 at Rabbit Ears Pass. 13.2 inches of snow was reported at Steamboat Springs 2.0ESE. Winds were generally below 45 mph with gusts in the 30s.

CZ_NAME: GORE AND ELK MOUNTAINS/CENTRAL MOUNTAIN VALLEYS
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Southwest flow ahead of a deepening cutoff low over the Desert Southwest allowed for sufficient moisture to work into the area along a strong cold front. An additional low pressure system dropped in from the northwest, bringing colder air with it and pushing this cold front across the area. This resulted in moderate to heavy snowfall across the central and southern mountains of western Colorado and southeast Utah with the higher accumulations over the Uncompahgre Plateau and San Juan Mountains due to colder air and favorable orographics.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 6 to 12 inches of snow fell across the area. A peak wind gust of 47 mph occurred at Snowmass Ski Area.

CZ_NAME: CENTRAL GUNNISON AND UNCOMPAHGRE RIVER BASIN
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Southwest flow ahead of a deepening cutoff low over the Desert Southwest allowed for sufficient moisture to work into the area along a strong cold front. An additional low pressure system dropped in from the northwest, bringing colder air with it and pushing this cold front across the area. This resulted in moderate to heavy snowfall across the central and southern mountains of western Colorado and southeast Utah with the higher accumulations over the Uncompahgre Plateau and San Juan Mountains due to colder air and favorable orographics.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 2 to 6 inches of snow fell across the area with locally higher amounts up to 9.3 inches of snow reported at Crawford 3.6SW.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An open wave low pressure trough moved through Utah and formed a closed low near the Four Corners before pushing through southwest Colorado. Significant moisture ahead of this trough and cold air behind a strong cold front led to moderate to heavy snowfall across the mountains of western Colorado and southeast Utah, with the highest snowfall totals across the Uncompahgre Gorge area of the northwest San Juans due to favorable light northerly flow behind the closed low and temperatures within the dendritic growth zone.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Douglas Pass reported 6.5 inches of snow. Winds gusted to 41 mph at Douglas Pass but sustained winds peaked to 32 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Potent surface moisture lingered within Colorado valleys overnight on January 2 and 3 resulting from widespread rain and snow over the prior two days. A deep upper-level ridge moving overhead made way for clear skies though. With the resulting radiational cooling, overnight surface conditions became saturated and supported widespread dense fog development.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread dense fog developed within the Grand Valley on the morning of January 3. The KGJT ASOS at the Grand Junction Regional Airport reported a brief period of 1/4-mile visibility from dense fog early in the morning before sunrise. Reduced visibility due to mist and fog remained before a prolonged period of less than 1/4 mile visibility due to dense fog developed after sunrise and lasted almost two hours. No reports of injuries, damages, or fatalities were received.

CZ_NAME: CENTRAL GUNNISON AND UNCOMPAHGRE RIVER BASIN
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Weak southwesterly flow around a deepening trough brought mid-level moisture over Colorado, prompting snow showers to portions of the region overnight from January 7 to 8. Some weak moisture from previous days' precipitation lingered in some valleys still, and brief breaks in cloud cover enabled enough radiational cooling to saturate some locations. Localized dense fog was able to develop as a result, though lingering cloud cover over central Colorado in the hours around sunrise kept fog development primarily to the lower valleys of western Colorado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Localized dense fog developed within the lower valleys along US-50 on the morning of January 8. The KMTJ ASOS at the Montrose Regional Airport reported about a half an hour of 1/4-mile visibility due to dense fog occurring between two snow showers shortly after sunrise. No reports of injuries, damages, or fatalities were received.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As the month of January continued to be generally drier and warmer than normal, drought began to expand outward from the core of Exceptional (D4) Drought in the central Colorado Divide.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: With the 1/21 US Drought Monitor update, an area of Extreme (D3) Drought had expanded into Colorado Zone 013 - Flattop Mountains. This area of Extreme (D3) Drought covered 10% of the zone, with 80% of the zone under Severe (D2) Drought, and 10% under Moderate (D1) Drought. These drought conditions remained unchanged through the end of the month. No reports of impacts or damages related to this drought expansion have been received.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Localized dense fog developed within the Lower Yampa River Basin on the morning of January 8. The K4V0 AWOS at the Rangely Airport reported multiple brief periods, an hour long or shorter duration, of 1/4-mile visibility due to freezing fog beginning shortly before sunrise and occurring for a couple hours after sunrise. No reports of injuries, damages, or fatalities were received.

CZ_NAME: GORE AND ELK MOUNTAINS/CENTRAL MOUNTAIN VALLEYS
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The winter of 2025-2026 has see precious little in the amount of snow accumulating in the mountains. When a decent storm does develop, bringing fresh snow to the higher elevations, back country skiers flock to the terrain. However, with the infrequency of accumulating snow this winter, the snowpack is overall unstable, and liable to avalanche when this new fresh snow loads on top of the older, unstable layers. At that point, all it takes is a person in the wrong place at the wrong time.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: To summarize the write up provided by the Colorado Avalanche Information Center (CAIC) regarding this incident: The area of the incident is known locally as King Arthur's/Timber Falls (39.628, -106.308). 1 backcountry tourer caught and partially buried, injured. Skier triggered avalanched rated as R2 and D2, meaning it was small relative to it's path but was large enough to catch, injure or kill a person. Avalanche was below treelline and on an east facing slope. CAIC had rated the avalanche danger in that area on that day a 2/5 or moderate, mainly at or above treeline, with danger ranged 1/5 or low below treeline. North through east facing slopes rated most dangerous. 2 skiers triggered the avalanche, with only one being caught and pinned against a tree, suffering cuts and abrasions. With assistance from the other skier, who was able to avoid the avalanche, the trapped skier was freed and both were able to leave the site of the avalanche.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep Pacific trough brought moist southwesterly flow to much of the western CONUS on January 1. A weak upper-level jet streak arrived in coincidence with the surge of moisture, prompting widespread precipitation in western Colorado throughout the morning of the 1st. Strong cold air advection behind the storms rapidly saturated valleys, and areas of dense fog developed during throughout the night and into the morning of January 2.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Patchy dense fog developed within the Lower Yampa River Basin early on the morning of January 2. The K4V0 AWOS at the Rangely Airport reported brief 1/4-mile visibility from freezing fog before sunrise. No reports of injuries, damages, or fatalities were received.

With the resulting radiational cooling, overnight surface conditions became saturated and supported widespread dense fog development.

The KMTJ ASOS at the Montrose Regional Airport reported 1/4-mile visibility due to freezing fog for about an hour near midnight. The KAJZ AWOS at the Blake Field Delta Airport reported multiple brief periods of reduced visibility prior to sunrise as well as over three hours of 1/4-mile visibility near and after sunrise due to dense fog. No reports of injuries, damages, or fatalities were received.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong moisture transport flowed through the Intermountain West around a deep upper-level trough in late January, bringing widespread precipitation to Colorado's west slope. While much of the region continued to be impacted by active weather through the night and morning, drying mid-levels cleared skies over the Grand and Paradox Valleys. Dense fog already began to develop at this point, but as the frontal boundary supporting the precipitation dipped south, cooling temperatures prolonged dense fog throughout the majority of the morning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread dense fog developed within the Grand Valley on the morning of January 24. The KGJT ASOS at the Grand Junction Regional Airport reported two prolonged periods of reduced visibility due to dense fog. The first period lasted about two and a half hours leading up to sunrise with visibilities reaching 1/4 mile. Fog dispersed for a short period before returning for about another two hours in which visibility dropped below 1/4 mile. No reports of injuries, damages, or fatalities were received.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moderate surge of moisture accompanied by a transient shortwave swept southeastwards through the Intermountain West on January 5 and 6, bringing rain and snow to the west slope of Colorado. Heavier bands of precipitation developed over northwestern Colorado valleys, leaving the near-surface environment saturated even as high pressure filled in throughout the night. As temperatures cooled with clearing skies, widespread dense fog developed within some of these northwestern Colorado valleys.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread, yet relatively brief, dense fog developed within the Central Yampa River Basin early on the morning of January 6. The KCAG ASOS at the Craig-Moffat Airport reported 1/4-mile visibility due to freezing fog for over two hours, and the KHDN AWOS at the Yampa Valley Regional Airport reported about an hour of 1/4-mile visibility due to freezing fog. No reports of injuries, damages, or fatalities were received.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An open low pressure trough moved through the area with better dynamics and moisture at the onset, with the Park Range Mountains favored for the better accumulations. Moderate snow continued across the central and southern Colorado mountains as well as the La Sal Mountains and Abajo Mountains of southeast Utah, driven mainly from orographic components. Temperatures were fairly mild, which limited snow accumulations to the higher elevations above 8000 feet.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 5 to 12 inches of snow fell across the area with locally higher amounts up to 24 inches at Tower. A peak wind gust of 45 mph occurred at Storm Peak Lab.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts of 59 and 61 mph were recorded from the Burlington Airport (KITR) ASOS. A personal weather station at 9 NNW Bethune recorded a gust of 58 mph. A personal weather station at 7 N Stratton recorded a gust of 57 mph.  A personal weather station recorded a gust of 56 mph at 9 NNW Bethune. A member of the public reported a semi overturned on I-70 east approximately 1 mile from Stratton, Colorado.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A jet streak and shortwave trough pushed through the northern and central portions of the area, bringing moderate snowfall to the northern and central Colorado mountains as well as the Eastern Uinta Mountains of northeast Utah. The strong southwest flow also led to windy conditions at the higher elevations resulting in blowing snow as well. Temperatures stayed warm with this system, keeping better snow accumulations mainly confined to areas above 9000 feet.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 6 to 10 inches of snow fell across the area with 7 inches reported at Skyway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An open wave low pressure trough moved through Utah and formed a closed low near the Four Corners before pushing through southwest Colorado. Significant moisture ahead of this trough and cold air behind a strong cold front led to moderate to heavy snowfall across the mountains of western Colorado and southeast Utah, with the highest snowfall totals across the Uncompahgre Gorge area of the northwest San Juans due to favorable light northerly flow behind the closed low and temperatures within the dendritic growth zone.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 7 to 13 inches of snow fell across the area with 12.5 inches reported at Skyway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A second day of high winds occurred across East Central CO.  The high winds started after 9 AM and continued through 4 PM.  The peak gust was 72 MPH recorded at the Burlington airport.  The loose soil and high winds caused a dust storm to occur on the west side of Yuma.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind speeds ranged from 58 to 67 MPH across the county, with the highest gust reported near Kit Carson from a Union Pacific weather sensor.

CZ_NAME: ALAMOSA VICINITY / CENTRAL SAN LUIS VALLEY BELOW 8500 FT
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An ejecting shortwave trough passed over the region during the second week of January, leading to quickly accumulating snow for the mountains and a rain/snow mix for the eastern plains of Colorado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There was 6.0 inches of snow measured.
